title: Post-punk, Industrial Culture Zines, and the Information Dark Age
author: Haworth

opening_move.description: Standard accounts of the internet's political trajectory (from communicative utopia to authoritarian 'dark age') treat the alt-right's online tactics as an exploitation of an originally democratic technology. Haworth argues instead that industrial musicians' zines, handbooks and websites of the 1980s–90s prefigured and helped produce the authoritarian, nihilistic information practices of the contemporary far right — questioning whether democratic utopia was ever the internet's 'default state'.
opening_move.stakes_claimed: Tracing the authoritarian-occult-nihilistic strain of post-punk zine culture (Vague, Rapid Eye, CCRU) to contemporary neoreaction reveals a genealogy that challenges both 'internet exceptionalism' and the narrative of democratic potential betrayed — the information dark age has deep subcultural roots.

rivals_engaged: Turner's account of the far right exploiting a liberal democratic internet
theoretical_positioning.description: Against the narrative of democratic internet corrupted by the right, Haworth proposes a genealogical alternative: industrial culture zines of the 1980s–90s developed authoritarian, nihilistic, and occult communicational aesthetics that directly prefigure contemporary neoreaction and the alt-right's media tactics. The CCRU (Nick Land) is the key mediating figure.

epistemic_mode.qualitative_methods: Close analysis and visual/textual interpretation of zines (Vague magazine, Rapid Eye) and websites (CCRU publications, industrial music handbooks); genealogical tracing from post-punk/industrial culture through to neoreaction and Dark Enlightenment.
epistemic_mode.interpretive_methods: Subcultural theory and media genealogy; visual culture analysis of zine aesthetics; intellectual history of industrial music's political-ideological evolution from post-punk toward authoritarian politics.
epistemic_mode.notes: The zines and CCRU texts are constitutive objects of analysis. The argument is that these media objects embody and transmit a specific authoritarian-nihilistic communicational aesthetic that links post-punk to contemporary far-right internet culture.

case_type.case_name: Industrial culture zines (Vague, Rapid Eye) and CCRU publications (1980s–1990s)

normative_stake.domain: media history, subcultural studies, internet politics

confidence_notes: This is a media genealogy paper with constitutive case material: the industrial culture zines and CCRU publications are the primary objects of analysis. The opening puzzle (dark age as deviation from democratic norm) is challenged by a counter-genealogy showing the 'dark' tendencies were present from the start. The CCRU / Nick Land connection to contemporary neoreaction is the key thread.
